text: We are convened on FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund, Ltd.. The question presented: Does Section 47(b) of the ICA, 15 U.S.C. § 80a-46 (b), create an implied private right of action? Private votes first, then statements.

I vote to reverse. The strongest reason is statutory text and structure. Under Sandoval, courts cannot imply a private right of action absent clear congressional intent. Section 47(b) declares contracts void but creates no private cause of action. Furthermore, the ICA’s express inclusion of private rights of action in other provisions, like § 36(b), demonstrates Congress knew how to create them when intended. We cannot rewrite this carefully calibrated statutory scheme. Because the text is silent on a private right of action for rescission under § 47(b), the lower court erred in implying one.

Analyst One, your structural point is strong, but how do you answer the opposing equitable argument? If a contract is statutorily 'void,' doesn't rescission merely restore the status quo without needing an express damages cause of action? Does the Second Circuit’s view that 'void' inherently permits private rescission survive Sandoval when seeking equitable relief? I lean reverse, but I ask the panel: can we dismiss the 'void' text simply because § 36(b) exists, or does 'void' carry an inherent equitable enforcement mechanism?

Analyst Three, you ask if 'void' inherently permits equitable rescission. But under Sandoval, the threshold question is whether Congress created a private cause of action, regardless of the remedy sought. Section 47(b) defines a contract's legal status but lacks text authorizing a private party to initiate a suit. If Congress intended private enforcement—even equitable—it would have specified it, as it did in § 36(b). We cannot bootstrap a private cause of action from a substantive definition of contract validity. I vote to reverse.

While the ICA’s investor protection goals and the equitable need to enforce 'void' contracts strongly support affirming, the statute's text and structure strongly support reversing. Analyst Three, while 'void' defines a contract's status, Sandoval requires a private right of action, not merely a private remedy. Congress explicitly created private rights in § 36(b) but omitted them in § 47(b). We cannot imply a statutory cause of action simply because a contract is void, nor bootstrap equitable rescission into a private right Congress withheld. Therefore, I vote to reverse.

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three asks if "void" inherently permits equitable rescission, but this conflates a contract's legal status with a private right of action. Under Sandoval, the threshold is whether Congress created the right to sue. Section 47(b) merely declares violative contracts void without authorizing private suits. Furthermore, the ICA’s express inclusion of private rights in § 36(b) proves Congress knows how to create them when intended. We cannot imply a private cause of action simply to enforce an equitable status quo when the statutory text and structure explicitly withhold it.

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three asks if 'void' inherently permits equitable rescission post-Sandoval. It does not. Sandoval requires Congress to create a private right to sue, regardless of the remedy sought. Section 47(b) merely defines a contract's legal status, providing no textual hook for a private plaintiff to initiate litigation. Furthermore, the ICA’s express private rights in § 36(b) confirm Congress knew how to authorize suits when intended. We cannot bootstrap an equitable cause of action from a substantive definition of contract validity when the statutory scheme deliberately omits it.

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three raises the strongest opposing point: that because Section 47(b) makes contracts 'void,' courts inherently have equitable power to rescind them without an express statutory cause of action. But Sandoval requires Congress to create the right to sue first. A contract being 'void' just describes its legal status; it doesn't grant a private citizen the power to drag someone into federal court. Since Congress included express private rights in Section 36(b) but omitted them here, we cannot imply a private right of action just to enforce an equitable status quo.

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three raises the best case for affirming: that declaring a contract 'void' inherently authorizes equitable rescission without an express cause of action. However, under Sandoval, the threshold is whether Congress created a private right to sue, not just a private remedy. Section 47(b) defines a contract's legal status but provides no textual hook for a private plaintiff to initiate litigation. Because Congress explicitly created private rights in § 36(b) but omitted them in § 47(b), we cannot bootstrap an equitable cause of action from a substantive definition of contract validity. The lower court erred.

I vote to reverse. Analyst Three raises the strongest counterargument: that because Section 47(b) declares contracts 'void,' courts inherently possess equitable authority to rescind them without an express cause of action. However, this conflates a court's general equitable powers with a statutory private right of action. Under Sandoval, the threshold is congressional intent to create the right to sue, not just the power to grant a remedy. Even if a contract is void, a private plaintiff still needs statutory authorization to initiate the suit. Since Section 47(b) lacks this text and Section 36(b) proves Congress knows how to provide it, we must reverse.

The disagreement centers on whether a statutory designation of a contract as void inherently invokes equitable authority for rescission, or whether precedent requires an express private right of action before any such remedy is available. This juxtaposes the equitable focus on restoring the status quo against the structural requirement for clear congressional intent to create a cause of action.

Analyst Eight, does § 36(b)'s express right truly negate equitable rescission, or does it merely provide an alternative damages remedy? If § 47(b) voids a contract, doesn't a court inherently possess equitable authority to rescind it without needing an express statutory cause of action for legal damages?

living_constitutionalist, raising a void contract as a defense requires no private right of action. But Saba seeks an *affirmative* cause of action. Under *Sandoval*, courts cannot imply affirmative private rights of action—equitable or legal—absent clear congressional intent. Section 47(b) provides no such text.

Analyst Eight, your defense versus sword distinction is sharp. But probing our reverse position: if § 47(b) explicitly declares these contracts 'void,' doesn't a court's inherent equitable power to rescind void agreements bypass *Sandoval*? Must Congress really use magic words to let a court undo a contract it already statutorily nullified?

Equitable power to void a contract defensively doesn't create an affirmative statutory cause of action. Sandoval requires congressional intent for the right to sue, not merely the underlying right. Section 47(b) provides a shield, not a sword. Courts cannot use inherent equitable powers to manufacture a private cause of action Congress deliberately omitted.

reason_text: The Equity Jurist's argument that Saba seeks equitable rescission of a void contract rather than statutory damages successfully distinguishes this claim from Sandoval, as courts possess inherent equitable authority to rescind void agreements without requiring an express statutory cause of action.
